summary: Rox presents a well-structured account report with clear data hierarchy and useful suggested actions. However, several accessibility concerns exist around color contrast, icon-only buttons lacking labels, and information conveyed through color alone in the tag system. The dual-panel layout is effective but the left panel's content hierarchy and some interactive affordances could be improved.

issues.title: Tag pills rely on color alone to convey category meaning
issues.location: Company attribute tags (Private/LP-owned, N/A, Founded 2015, etc.) in the right panel header
issues.problem: The beige/tan colored tag pills use a subtle background color to distinguish them from surrounding content, but there is no additional visual encoding (icons, borders, or grouping labels) to differentiate tag categories. Users with color vision deficiencies may not perceive the distinction between tags and plain text.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.4.1 requires that color is not used as the sole means of conveying information. Without redundant cues, colorblind users lose the grouping semantics of these tags.
issues.recommendation: Add category labels or icons before tag groups (e.g., 'Type: Private/LP-owned', 'Location: SF, CA, USA'). Alternatively, use distinct border styles or icons within each tag to reinforce meaning beyond color.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.1 Use of Color (A): information must not be conveyed by color alone

issues.title: Icon-only action buttons lack accessible labels
issues.location: Bottom-left of the chat panel — speaker, copy, and refresh icons
issues.problem: Three icon-only buttons (audio/speaker, copy, refresh) appear without visible text labels or tooltips. Screen reader users will not know the purpose of these controls, and sighted users may also struggle to identify exact functionality.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.1.1 requires all non-text content to have a text alternative. Icon-only buttons without aria-labels create barriers for assistive technology users and violate the non-text content requirement.
issues.recommendation: Add aria-label attributes to each icon button (e.g., 'Read aloud', 'Copy response', 'Regenerate response'). Additionally, implement visible tooltips on hover/focus to assist all users.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A): all non-text content must have a text alternative

issues.title: Low contrast on 'Thought for 33s' and subtitle text
issues.location: 'Thought for 33s' label and placeholder text in the chat input field
issues.problem: The light gray 'Thought for 33s' text and the 'Ask about the selected account' placeholder appear to have insufficient contrast against the white/light background, likely falling below the 4.5:1 minimum ratio for normal-sized text. This makes the text difficult to read for users with low vision.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.4.3 mandates a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text. Low-contrast text increases cognitive load and excludes users with visual impairments.
issues.recommendation: Increase the text color darkness to achieve at least 4.5:1 contrast ratio. For the placeholder text, use a color like #767676 or darker. For the 'Thought for 33s' label, use at least medium gray (#595959).
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): normal text requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio

issues.title: Left sidebar icons lack text labels and visible focus indicators
issues.location: Left vertical navigation sidebar with icon-only items
issues.problem: The sidebar contains approximately 8 icon-only navigation items with no visible text labels. There is no indication of the active state beyond a subtle highlight, and keyboard focus indicators are not visible in the screenshot. Users navigating by keyboard cannot determine their current location.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.4.7 requires a visible focus indicator for keyboard navigation. Additionally, icon-only navigation without labels violates discoverability principles, increasing the gulf of execution for new users.
issues.recommendation: Add tooltip labels on hover/focus for each icon. Implement a clearly visible focus ring (e.g., 2px solid outline with offset) for keyboard navigation. Consider adding an expanded sidebar option with text labels.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA): keyboard focus indicator must be visible

issues.title: Chart bars lack direct data labels, requiring estimation
issues.location: Revenue Growth: 2023–2025 bar chart in the right panel
issues.problem: The bar chart shows three revenue bars but only the 2023 bar has a text annotation ('2023: ~$2B'). The 2024 and 2025 bars require users to estimate values from the Y-axis gridlines. This forces unnecessary cognitive work and reduces data precision.
issues.critical_reason: Per the signal-to-noise ratio principle, key data should be immediately accessible without requiring mental calculation. Users scanning a report expect precise figures directly labeled on or near the visual elements.
issues.recommendation: Add data labels above or inside each bar showing the revenue value (e.g., '~$7B' for 2024, '~$13-20B' for 2025). This eliminates guesswork and supports quick scanning behavior.
issues.reference: Universal Principles of Design — Signal-to-Noise Ratio: maximise relevant information, minimise clutter

issues.title: Static Website preview card has truncated title with no access to full content
issues.location: 'Static Website' preview card showing 'OpenAI Interactive Account R...' with truncated text
issues.problem: The preview card title is truncated with an ellipsis ('OpenAI Interactive Account R...') and the content below appears as placeholder loading bars rather than actual content. Users cannot determine what the full content is without clicking through, and the loading skeleton may be confused with actual content.
issues.critical_reason: According to Norman's Gulf of Evaluation, users must be able to determine the system's current state. Truncated text and ambiguous skeleton screens create uncertainty about whether content has loaded or what the preview actually contains.
issues.recommendation: Show the full title or allow wrapping to two lines. If the content is still loading, add a clear loading indicator with text (e.g., 'Loading preview...'). If these are placeholder lines by design, make them look distinct from loading states.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Gulf of Evaluation: user cannot tell if their action worked

issues.title: Suggested actions have low visual affordance as clickable elements
issues.location: Three suggested action items below 'Suggested actions' label
issues.problem: The suggested actions appear as lightly bordered text blocks with a small arrow icon. Their visual treatment is similar to static content, and the arrow affordance is subtle. Users may not immediately recognize these as interactive clickable elements.
issues.critical_reason: Norman's Affordance principle states that interactive elements must visually communicate how they are used. When buttons look like static text blocks, users hesitate or miss available actions entirely, increasing the gulf of execution.
issues.recommendation: Increase the visual affordance by adding a hover state preview, using a more prominent button-like styling (e.g., slightly stronger border, background color change on hover), or adding a clear 'Click to execute' icon treatment.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Affordance: element must communicate how it is used

issues.title: KPI cards lack sufficient visual differentiation from each other
issues.location: Four metric cards (Revenue, Users, Infra Investment, Last updated) in the right panel
issues.problem: All four KPI cards share the same beige background color and card styling, making it harder to quickly scan and locate a specific metric. The cards blend together visually despite containing categorically different types of information (financial, user, infrastructure, metadata).
issues.critical_reason: Gestalt's Law of Similarity suggests that elements sharing identical visual properties are perceived as belonging to the same category. When different data types look the same, scanning efficiency drops and users must read each card sequentially.
issues.recommendation: Differentiate cards using subtle color coding, distinct icons for each category, or vary the card background slightly. For example, use a green tint for growth metrics and a neutral tint for metadata like 'Last updated'.

issues.title: Dual-panel layout may cause confusion about primary content focus
issues.location: Overall page layout — chat panel (left) and report panel (right)
issues.problem: The two-panel layout gives roughly equal visual weight to the chat interface and the generated report. New users may be uncertain which panel to focus on first, and the relationship between the chat input and the report output is not explicitly communicated.
issues.critical_reason: According to Hick's Law, presenting multiple areas of equal prominence slows decision-making. Users need a clear visual hierarchy to understand the primary content area and the workflow direction.
issues.recommendation: Consider making the report panel slightly more prominent (wider ratio like 60/40) or adding a visual connector/breadcrumb showing the flow: 'Prompt → Generated Report'. A subtle panel header distinction would also help establish reading order.
issues.reference: Psychology of Design — Hick's Law: too many choices slows decisions

issues.title: Publish button lacks confirmation for irreversible action
issues.location: 'Publish' button in the top-right corner of the report panel
issues.problem: The prominent red/orange 'Publish' button appears to make the report publicly available but lacks any indication of a confirmation step. Accidentally clicking this could share incomplete or inaccurate AI-generated data externally.
issues.critical_reason: Norman's Forcing Function principle states that irreversible or high-consequence actions should include confirmation barriers to prevent errors. Publishing potentially inaccurate AI-generated content without review creates reputational risk.
issues.recommendation: Add a confirmation dialog before publishing (e.g., 'Are you sure you want to publish this report? It will be visible to your team.'). Include a preview step or draft state indicator to make the current status clear.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Forcing Function: prevent irreversible actions with confirmation
